items.title: Exploring Downtown: Chattanooga City Hall Completed in 1908, Chattanooga City Hall has served as the city's municipal ce...
items.content_text:
Exploring Downtown: Chattanooga City Hall
 Completed in 1908, Chattanooga City Hall has served as the city's municipal center for more than a century. Designed by architect R. H. Hunt in the Classical Revival style, the building reflects an architectural movement that shaped many of Downtown Chattanooga's civic buildings.
 Its symmetrical design and prominent columns draw inspiration from the public buildings of ancient Greece and Rome, a style often associated with civic duty, democracy, and permanence. 
 Today, City Hall remains one of Downtown Chattanooga's most recognizable civic landmarks. It was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1980.

items.content_text:
Some of life's biggest milestones begin in unexpected places.  

For Randy and Brandee Coppinger, Komatsu North America is where they met, built careers together and raised their family.  More than 30 years later, they're still part of the team that has helped make Komatsu Chattanooga what it is today.  

Their story is a reminder that the strongest companies are built by strong relationships, both on and off the job. 

#Komatsu40 #BuiltByChattanooga

items.title: HCSO SWAT Unit Successfully Apprehends Aggravated Assault Suspect Hamilton County, TN – On the evening of July 20, 2026,...
items.content_text:
HCSO SWAT Unit Successfully Apprehends Aggravated Assault Suspect
 Hamilton County, TN – On the evening of July 20, 2026, the Soddy-Daisy Police Department requested the help of HCSO deputies after an aggravated assault suspect refused to exit a residence at the 8400 block of Springfield Road.
 The Soddy-Daisy Police Department believed the suspect, Jerry Eustice Jr., to be armed, and Eustice has a history of violent behavior. The HCSO SWAT Unit responded to the scene, securing the area to protect the neighbors and successfully taking Eustice into custody. He was turned over to the Soddy-Daisy Police Department for further investigation.
 This reflects the mission of the HCSO SWAT Unit: to preserve life during high-risk situations through specialized training, equipment, and tactics.
 All questions regarding the aggravated assault investigation or criminal charges should be directed to the Soddy-Daisy Police Department.
 This information is preliminary and subject to change. No further details are available.

items.title: State enterprises play a major role in industrial subsidies. Companies with more than 25% government ownership tend to r...
items.content_text:
State enterprises play a major role in industrial subsidies.
 Companies with more than 25% government ownership tend to receive relatively more:
  ➜ Government grants
  ➜ Below-market borrowings 
 This is especially common in heavy industries, where firms depend more heavily on debt financing and support from state banks.
 Learn more about the role of state enterprises in industrial subsidies: https://brnw.ch/21x4isF

items.title: What a wonderful evening at the 91st Annual Chattanooga Ball! Keith and I were honored to be invited to sit at the King’...
items.content_text:
What a wonderful evening at the 91st Annual Chattanooga Ball!
 Keith and I were honored to be invited to sit at the King’s table as we celebrated King Alan Shuptrine. It was such a joy to support Alan and his beautiful wife, Bonny, who have given so much of themselves to our community over the years. Their generosity and servant hearts are a blessing to so many, and this recognition was truly well deserved.
 Congratulations to King Alan Shuptrine, Queen Reese Hullander, the Belles, and everyone who helped make the evening so special. It was wonderful to spend the evening with friends, celebrate a great Chattanooga tradition, and see so many people come together to give back to our community.
 Alan and Bonny, thank you for your gracious hospitality. Keith and I had a wonderful time and were grateful to celebrate this special honor with you both. ❤️

items.title: Thank you for the birthday wishes! At first I struggled with turning an age closer to 50, but my son Blaize, Tenice, and...
items.content_text:
Thank you for the birthday wishes! At first I struggled with turning an age closer to 50, but my son Blaize, Tenice, and Chasyn called and we actually figured it wasn't so bad aging. It only means I am getting closer to that elder status and the benefits that come with it 😆 anywho... Spent my birthday heading to TNSTRONG YOUTH SUMMIT with a few of our amazing Unicoi SADD students, Greeneville County and Johnson County crews. If I can't be with my family, might as well spend it with my extended family 😁 
 But before my send off, my girls Ten, Evie, and TC cooked me breakfast, and my daughter made me a beautiful picture 🥹 along with a basket full of goodies, and got my boys to eat some breakfast with me. The Coalition crews and SADD girls got me some gifts as well. Feeling the Love!

items.title: Today, the City of Chattanooga and Hamilton County launched Bring Back the BLVD — an initiative that will spark the revi...
items.content_text:
Today, the City of Chattanooga and Hamilton County launched Bring Back the BLVD — an initiative that will spark the revitalization of one of Chattanooga’s main gateways! 
 We are advocating to make this area an Opportunity Zone under the State of Tennessee’s Opportunity Zones 2.0 program, which if approved, creates federal tax incentives to encourage private investment in existing properties, transforming vacant and underutilized sites. The Opportunity Zone recommendation now moves to the State for consideration.

items.title: 🎉Special Exhibit🎉 "What If: Designs from the Future of Crafting & Growing" August 8, 2026
items.content_text:
🎉Special Exhibit🎉
 "What If: Designs from the Future of Crafting & Growing"
 August 8, 2026 - September 26, 2026
 Opening Reception and Artist Talk August 8 at 1:30pm
 FREE During Museum Hours
 An exhibit by Humanities Tennessee’s Shared Futures Lab, features artifacts and stories from the future imagined by seven Tennessee artists and farmers about their work. 
 The speculative designs invite the public to imagine the broad impact of possible change–from sports to building, burial to celebrity–and how it may shape our future communities.
 Curated by
 Liza Blair
 Curator and Museum Specialist
 Denise Heinly Art Center and Outdoor Museum of Art
 Chattanooga State Community College
 “Imagining Future Stories” Opening Reception and Artist Talk 
 August 8, 2026 | 1:30pm - 3pm
 FREE
 Join What If…? curator Liza Blair in conversation with crafters & growers for a deeper dive into the future stories they imagined and the artifacts and ideas that inhabit them.
 Panelists include:
 Julia Donagrandi, Loof'n Round Gardens, (Loofahs Grown to Enhance Our Home | Loof'n Round Gardens)
 Annie LaVoie, Mouthy Oddities, Home | Life is beautiful, even after death.™
 Sheila-Renese Williams, Moore Family Century Farm. Moore Family Century Farm | schedule farm tour | Holladay, TN, USA
 Sponsored by Humanities Tennessee and TN Arts Commission.

Two UTC College of Nursing faculty members recently shared their work on AI-assisted simulation with an international audience, leading a webinar on how the technology can help prepare graduate nurse practitioner students for difficult conversations in palliative care.
 Dr. Chris Doneski, director of UTC’s acute care nurse practitioner program and a simulation faculty member, and Dr. Rosebelle Peters, nursing simulation program director and senior lecturer, presented “Prepare Graduate Nurse Practitioners for Difficult Conversations with AI Palliative Simulations” through HealthySimulation.com.
 🔗  Learn more here: https://blog.utc.edu/…/difficult-discussions-how-utc-nursi…/

items.title: After decades of searching and speculation, we finally found him!  Skeptics left aghast after Bigfoot was discovered in...
items.content_text:
After decades of searching and speculation, we finally found him! 
 Skeptics left aghast after Bigfoot was discovered in a local park! We are lucky he stopped and posed for a quick photo op before scurrying off like Jimothy the raccoon, his inspiration and idol.
 Our staff have affectionately named this hunk of metal Freddie the Yeti, and he's looking for his owner. If you know who left him at the park, please reach out to us so we can safely get him back home. This is why you should always microchip your pets!
 While we are grateful for his patronage to our parks, we can't allow him to loiter. We have a search and rescue operation scheduled for this evening, and he will seek temporary solace in our lost and found at Outdoor Chattanooga. We look forward to a tearful reunification. 
 Help us share this post so it reaches his owner! If you're the owner, please DM us with Freddy's posted location and we will get him back to you, or come to Outdoor Chattanooga starting tomorrow, 7/21, with proof of ownership (vet records, baby photos, etc...) to claim.

items.title: A new chapter for one of Chattanooga’s historic corridors? Hamilton County Mayor Weston Wamp, Chattanooga Mayor Tim Kell...
items.content_text:
A new chapter for one of Chattanooga’s historic corridors?
 Hamilton County Mayor Weston Wamp, Chattanooga Mayor Tim Kelly, and community leaders have launched Bring Back the BLVD, an initiative focused on attracting investment, supporting local businesses, and restoring Rossville Boulevard as a thriving commercial corridor.
 For generations, Rossville Boulevard has been more than just a road — it has been a connection between communities, commerce, and history. When dedicated in April 1912, it was considered the city's finest stretch of roadway. A parade marked the occasion. In the 1920's, a portion of the Boulevard was designated a "Road of Remembrance" to honor soldiers. Two stone columns at 32nd Street still mark the entrance. The bronze plaque reads "FOUR AMERICAN ARMIES HAVE MARCHED OVER THIS ROAD. TWO OPPOSING ARMIES IN THE CIVIL WAR 1861-1865. ONE IN THE SPANISH AMERICAN WAR 1898. AND THE LAST IN THE WORLD WAR 1917-1918." Over the decades, the Boulevard became home to neighborhood businesses, industries, auto dealerships, and gathering places that reflected the diversity and entrepreneurial spirit of our city.
 Like many historic commercial corridors across America, Rossville Boulevard has faced challenges as development patterns changed. The proposed Opportunity Zone designation could help bring new attention and resources to this important gateway while honoring the people, businesses, and stories that have shaped the Boulevard for more than a century.
 At Preserve Chattanooga, we believe historic places are not just about the past, they are about creating stronger communities for the future. We look forward to seeing how preservation, investment, and community vision can work together to bring back the BLVD.
 Read more about this exciting initiative: https://www.chattanoogan.com/…/City-County-Partnering-To-Re…
 #PreserveChattanooga #HistoricChattanooga #RossvilleBoulevard #BringBackTheBLVD

items.content_text:
🌧️🚜 Stormwater Team Hard at Work After the Weekend Storms 🚜🌧️
 After the heavy rainfall that Red Bank experienced over the weekend, the City's Public Works Stormwater team has been working to assess drainage infrastructure and remove accumulated debris along Stringers Branch. This helps to restore the stream to its full flow capacity and reduce the risk of future blockages occurring, which can cause localized flooding. 
 If you notice a clogged drain, blocked ditch, or excessive debris in our public drainage infrastructure, please let us know! If you see something that our team needs to respond to, you can submit a work order at https://redbanktn.portal.iworq.net/portalhome/redbanktn or call 423-805-7059. Reporting these issues helps us to respond quickly and ensures that we're able to protect our community from flooding.
 Thank you to our Stormwater team for their dedication and hard work in keeping Red Bank's waterways and drainage infrastructure functioning properly after severe weather. 
 #RedBankTN #PublicWorks #StormwaterManagement #FloodPrevention

items.content_text:
The future of manufacturing isn't coming—it's already here. The question is: Are you ready for it?
 At ChattState, our Industrial Automation & Robotics Technician program prepares you to build, maintain, and troubleshoot the advanced systems that keep modern industry moving. From robotics and PLCs to automation controls, hydraulics, pneumatics, and Industry 4.0 technologies, you'll gain hands-on skills employers are looking for.
 Whether you're starting a new career or leveling up your technical skills, ChattState's TCAT can help you become the technician industries depend on.
 Financial aid including free tuition available to those who qualify. 
 Build your career now at chattanoogastate.edu/tcat  
 Video description: Two ChattState students work in the TCAT industrial lab with electrical boards and robotics.

items.title: Today was a big day for the Rossville Boulevard district. Our company is still considered part of this area from the int...
items.content_text:
Today was a big day for the Rossville Boulevard district. Our company is still considered part of this area from the interstate to the state line. But what makes this almost as exciting is that our original location on McFarland, where we were before I was born, touches this area as well. When this group first met, it was a very small group of us at The Barn Nursery. Today there was a press release with both mayors and every local news outlet.  
 Huge thanks to our commissioner and our wonderful council woman and both mayors for coming together on this. We are excited to see public and private investment, especially as we plan our company’s investments year by year that we continue to make in TBN and our surrounding community! Tim Kelly Weston Wamp Raquetta Dotley Jim Webster Cindy Watkins Webster Jody Shea Coralee Buckels Bryan Evans Lumber Crabtree Farms of Chattanooga

items.title: Thank you to everyone who joined us in Sale Creek to help shape Tennessee's 70th State Park. Last week, Tennessee State...
items.content_text:
Thank you to everyone who joined us in Sale Creek to help shape Tennessee's 70th State Park.
 Last week, Tennessee State Parks hosted two engagement opportunities as part of the master planning process for the future park at the McDonald Farm site. We met with local and state elected officials representing Hamilton and Rhea counties to hear perspectives on community priorities and regional needs. That evening, more than 100 community members attended the public open house to share ideas for the park's future.
 The feedback we received will help inform the master plan, which provides a long-term vision for protecting the park's natural and cultural resources while guiding future investments. We appreciate everyone who took the time to participate and help shape the future of this special place.
 If you were not able to attend, there is still time to share your feedback. An online survey is available through the end of August, and we encourage you to participate. Your input will help shape Tennessee's 70th State Park. The survey link is available in the comments.

items.content_text:
We need YOUR help putting a mural here! 🖌️
 AIM Center is looking for a professional mural artist (or artist team!) to help transform the exterior of Espero Chattanooga into a beautiful, hope-filled work of art.
 The selected artist will co-create alongside AIM Center members to design a mural that reflects the spirit of Espero, meaning "hope" in Spanish, and celebrates recovery, resilience, belonging, community, and the life-changing impact of safe, affordable housing.
 Artists from Chattanooga and the surrounding Appalachian region (TN, GA & AL), especially those with lived experience of mental illness, are encouraged to apply.
 🗓 Applications are due Friday, July 24 at 4:00 PM EST.
 Review the full RFQ, project details, eligibility requirements, and application instructions by clicking here ➡ https://aimcenterinc.org/…/espero-chattanooga-mural-project/

items.title: SCAM ALERT Financial-related scams have existed much longer than mobile payment apps, but these apps give scammers anoth...
items.content_text:
SCAM ALERT
 Financial-related scams have existed much longer than mobile payment apps, but these apps give scammers another avenue for taking money. Recently we received reports from people who got calls from someone pretending to be with Chime, telling them to download the app and transfer money to it. Chime, like many other financial technology companies, states they won't call urging you to send funds or share sensitive information, such as a Social Security number. 
 If you spot a scam or suspect your mobile payment app is hacked, immediately contact the app's customer service, and then follow up with the FTC and CPD (if you reside in the city limits) to file reports. Never give out passwords, PINs, or one-time login codes.

items.title: Italy’s growth has been resilient to the shocks of recent years, reducing unemployment and bringing more adults into the...
items.content_text:
Italy’s growth has been resilient to the shocks of recent years, reducing unemployment and bringing more adults into the workforce.
 But an ageing workforce, weak productivity growth, high public debt and growing competition from emerging market economies are posing challenges.
 Italy is making important progress through significant structural reforms and investments.
 Looking ahead, Italy must sustain reform momentum. Priorities include consolidating public finances, boosting business dynamism, increasing opportunities for young people and improving energy security.
 Learn more about the #OECD Economic Survey of Italy: https://brnw.ch/21x4gyP

The work to build a new nation has already begun. The only question is: Have you joined in?
 Building power doesn't happen overnight. It takes sustained investment, trusted relationships, and the resources to support organizations that show up for their communities every single day.
 Our 2025 Impact Report is proof of what's possible when we invest in Black communities year-round. Together with 451 grassroots partners across 20 states, we invested more than $4.3 million to strengthen local leadership, expand civic engagement, support mutual aid, and build lasting community power.
 As midterm elections approach, this work becomes even more critical, but it can't happen without resources. Every donation helps fund local organizations, equip organizers, and build the infrastructure needed to create lasting change.
 Support our movement: https://blackvotersmatterfund.org/donate/ 
 Read the full report: bit.ly/bvmimpact  
 #BlackVotersMatter #ImpactReport #CommunityPower #GrassrootsOrganizing

items.content_text:

Good Trouble Lives On: National Weekend of Action for Civic Engagement and Voting Rights | Atlanta, Ga.
 Hundreds of Georgians gather at Big Bethel A.M.E. Church as part of Good Trouble Lives On, a nationwide weekend of action honoring the life and legacy of Congressman John Lewis through peaceful, nonviolent civic engagement.
 Atlanta is one of more than 600 communities across the country participating in coordinated actions during the July 17–19 weekend, bringing together faith leaders, civil rights advocates, grassroots organizations, and community members committed to strengthening democracy through voter education, voter registration, and civic participation.
 At a time when new voting restrictions and changing election rules threaten to create confusion and barriers for millions of Americans, this year’s mobilization focuses on ensuring communities have the information and resources they need to participate fully in our democracy.
 Grounded in the theme “Teach! Reach! Preach!”, the Atlanta gathering will combine civic education, faith-based organizing, and community outreach to help voters understand new voting requirements, encourage voter registration, and inspire continued engagement beyond Election Day.
 Inspired by Congressman Lewis’s lifelong commitment to nonviolent action and the protection of voting rights, organizers say the weekend is about carrying forward his belief that democracy depends on ordinary people taking extraordinary action on behalf of one another.
 “This weekend is about more than honoring John Lewis’ legacy—it is about living it,” organizers said. “Across the country, people are coming together to educate, organize, and ensure every eligible voter has the opportunity to make their voice heard.”
 The event is strictly nonpartisan and does not support or oppose any candidate or political party.
 Download the Black Star Network app at http://www.blackstarnetwork.com! We're on iOS, AppleTV, Android, AndroidTV, Roku, FireTV, and SamsungTV.

items.title: How much do farmers earn around the world? On average, an agricultural worker earns about USD 3 800 per year globally an...
items.content_text:
How much do farmers earn around the world?
 On average, an agricultural worker earns about USD 3 800 per year globally and this is projected to rise by 9% over the next decade. However, this global average hides large differences between regions.
 In high‑income countries, workers earn much more, about USD 21 100 per year today. This is projected to rise to roughly USD 22 155 by 2035.
 In middle‑income countries, incomes are growing as agriculture becomes more mechanised and commercialised, though levels differ widely.
 In low‑income regions, farmers earn far less, around USD 930 per year, with only a modest increase to about USD 1 100 expected.
 Learn more in the OECD-FAO Agricultural Outlook: https://brnw.ch/21x4fhY
 #AgOutlook

items.title: Average annual economic losses from wildfires in #OECD countries nearly quadrupled between 2000-2014 and 2015-2024. Natu...
items.content_text:
Average annual economic losses from wildfires in #OECD countries nearly quadrupled between 2000-2014 and 2015-2024.
 Natural hazards, such as wildfires, can lead to huge financial costs that many individuals, households or businesses may struggle to cover.
 Insurance can provide an important source of funding to absorb these costs and support recovery.
 But with increasing wildfire risks driven by changes in land-use and weather conditions, economic losses are dramatically increasing, affecting access to affordable property insurance coverage in several regions.
 How can governments, insurance regulators and the insurance sector respond to these risks to ensure people are financially protected?
 Find out in the OECD report on Financial Protection Against Catastrophic Risks ➡️ https://brnw.ch/21x4eZ1

items.title: We recently hosted a Lunch & Learn in partnership with Kubota Tractor Corporation to showcase the latest innovations in...
items.content_text:
We recently hosted a Lunch & Learn in partnership with Kubota Tractor Corporation to showcase the latest innovations in Kubota equipment to improve efficiency, safety, and productivity in the field for CHAPublicWorks employees as well as other attendees from surrounding local municipalities at the quarterly APWA meeting. The presentation highlighted features and engineering improvements that lend toward increased operator comfort, improved visibility, simplified maintenance access, advanced hydraulic systems, and integrated technology to maximize uptime and reduce operating costs. 
 Kubota equipment showcased included:
 🔸KX080-5 
 🔸SVL97-3 
 🔸RTV-X Long Bed
 🔸Tractor Mid-Mount Boom Mower
 Participants also had the opportunity to climb into the operator stations, discuss real-world municipal applications, and compare equipment capabilities based on operational needs with an on site demonstration in the equipment yard. 
 This event reinforces the importance of strong partnerships between equipment providers, manufacturers, and Public Works agencies to provide valuable insight into equipment selection and best practices that support efficient municipal operations. We appreciate the opportunity to highlight Kubota equipment features and capabilities to the City of Chattanooga Public Works team, and we look forward to growing our partnership with them for many years in the future! 
 #kubotausa #chattanoogapublicworks #kubotaofchattanooga

A new inclusive playground could soon become a reality on Signal Mountain.
 This week, Mayor Weston Wamp, District 2 Commissioner Chip Baker, and county leaders proposed a $210,516 investment to help complete the Dahlia Playground at Althaus Park. Pending Hamilton County Commission approval, the funding will support the purchase of inclusive play equipment, creating a space where children of all abilities can play side by side.
 Inspired by Dahlia “Dollie” Harmon and her family’s vision for a more inclusive community, the project is another example of what strong public-private partnerships can accomplish for Hamilton County families.
 If approved on August 5, construction is expected to begin later this year. 🛝

Do your elected officials represent your values?
 That's a question only you can answer, but it starts with knowing the decisions they're making.
 From budgets and school policies to healthcare, housing, elections, and public safety, our elected officials make choices that shape our community every day. Those choices should reflect the values and priorities of those they seek to represent. 
 This post isn't about telling you what to think. It's about encouraging you to ask questions.
 ✔ What decisions have your elected officials made?
 ✔ Why did they make them?
 ✔ Do those decisions align with your values?
 An informed vote starts long before Election Day! 
 Early voting starts TODAY for our County General and State Primary Election! 
 Check out our School Board Voter Guide and Pledge to Vote on our website: www.calebcha.org/VOTE

Leaders from across Tennessee gathered to launch TN QuantumWorks, a first-of-its-kind K-12 quantum education initiative that will help prepare students for the jobs of tomorrow.
 Mayor Weston Wamp joined Mayor Tim Kelly, Senator Bo Watson, Hamilton County Schools, UTC, EPB, and community partners to celebrate Hamilton County’s role as the pilot district for this groundbreaking program.
 A special thank you to Dr. Dane and Sheila Boyington of Thinking Media for their vision and leadership in developing the innovative curriculum that will introduce students to quantum technology and future career opportunities.

items.title: The world is watching Spain — and now they're watching Chattanooga! Since January and the announcement of being Spain's...
items.content_text:
The world is watching Spain — and now they're watching Chattanooga! Since January and the announcement of being Spain's host city, we have talked to news outlets across the globe, been mentioned on social media, and had "Chattanooga" spoken corners of the world for the very first time. These news clips are just a small glimpse into the conversation.
 The $600,000 we spent from hotel-motel tax for Spain's security has generated over $100 million in ad value. While this remarkable return on our investment is worthy of celebration, the real excitement comes from knowing we have succeeded in getting the world talking about us. It might be about fútbol right now, but this momentum is how we make waves as a city — attracting visitors, advocating for funds that can make everyday life here better, and showing folks that we are not just some small town!

items.title: CPD Arrests Suspect in Manor Road Homicide The Chattanooga Police Department (CPD) arrested a man in connection to a hom...
items.content_text:
CPD Arrests Suspect in Manor Road Homicide
 The Chattanooga Police Department (CPD) arrested a man in connection to a homicide at a Manor Road residence.
 On July 16, 2026 at 3:32 p.m., CPD officers responded to a call regarding a deceased man in the 3900 block of Manor Road. 
 Upon arrival, police found the 53-year-old victim inside a residence with an injury to the back of his head.
 CPD's Homicide Unit responded to the scene to conduct the investigation and subsequently identified and arrested the suspect, 37-year-old Nathaniel Colvin. Colvin is charged with first-degree murder, especially aggravated robbery, and tampering with evidence. He is in custody at the Hamilton County Jail and Detention Center. 
 The information is preliminary and is subject to change as the investigation continues. Chattanooga Police ask anyone with information regarding this incident to call 423-698-2525.
 The charges and allegations referenced in this release are merely accusations of criminal conduct and not evidence. A defendant is presumed innocent unless and until proven guilty beyond a reasonable doubt and convicted through due process of law.
 #CPDnews

items.title: 🌿 Preserve the Land. Protect the Play. 🌿 Wildwood Playscape was created to inspire imagination, adventure, and a love...
items.content_text:
🌿 Preserve the Land. Protect the Play. 🌿
 Wildwood Playscape was created to inspire imagination, adventure, and a love for nature. Every log to climb, every trail to explore, and every space to discover exists because our community values places where families can connect and children can simply be kids.
 Recently, Wildwood experienced vandalism. It's incredibly disappointing to see a place built for everyone intentionally damaged, especially one that has quickly become a favorite destination for so many families.
 But we also know this community.
 Time and again, we've seen Hamilton County rally around the places, people, and causes it believes in. We know Wildwood is no different.
 We're asking everyone who visits to help us protect this special place. Respect the space, encourage the next generation to do the same, and if you see vandalism or destructive behavior, please report it. Together, we can ensure Wildwood remains a place of adventure, wonder, and lasting memories for generations to come.
 💚 Preserve the Land. Protect the Play.
 #ProtectThePlay #WildwoodPlayscape #EnterpriseSouth #HamiltonCountyParks

Thank you to our wonderful library mom, Meghan, for sharing her story with us.
 Watching Sam write his name for the very first time at the Downtown Library was an unforgettable milestone for their family, and a special moment for our staff, too.
 We’re grateful Meghan trusted us to share this memory as we celebrate 50 years of the Downtown Library. It’s moments like these that remind us how libraries become part of a family’s story.
 Do you have a special memory from the Downtown Library? Whether you’re a parent, grandparent, caregiver, or lifelong library lover, we’d love to hear your story! Share it in the comments or tag someone who has made meaningful memories at the Downtown Library.
 Looking for fun ways to help your little one build early literacy skills? Join us for Baby Bounce, Toddler Time, Preschool Storytime, and more! Visit the link in our bio to learn more about our free early literacy programs.

items.title: Meet the inaugural Hamilton County–East Ridge Sports Authority. Hamilton County appointees Dane Bradshaw, Katie Burrows,...
items.content_text:
Meet the inaugural Hamilton County–East Ridge Sports Authority.
 Hamilton County appointees Dane Bradshaw, Katie Burrows, Hiren Desai, and Orlando Lightfoot will serve alongside East Ridge appointees Zach Hutcherson, Brad Jackson, Ken Meyer, and PJ Patel, with Emerson Russell serving as the joint appointee.
 The Hamilton County Commission this week considered the resolution to establish the nine-member authority, which will guide the planning process by evaluating successful facility models, developing governance recommendations, and preparing a financing and operational plan before any construction moves forward.
 “The caliber of people serving on this authority—including former collegiate and professional athletes, business leaders, and hospitality experts—reflects our commitment to making Hamilton County a premier youth sports destination,” said Mayor Weston Wamp.

items.content_text: The HCSO conducted a 40-hour Law Enforcement Instructor Development Course designed to strengthen instructional skills, improve classroom delivery, and prepare officers to effectively train law enforcement personnel. The course was instructed by members of the HCSO Training & Professional Development Unit.

items.title: A new inclusive playground could soon become a reality on Signal Mountain. This week, Mayor Weston Wamp, District 2 Comm...
items.content_text:
A new inclusive playground could soon become a reality on Signal Mountain.
 This week, Mayor Weston Wamp, District 2 Commissioner Chip Baker, and county leaders proposed a $210,516 investment to help complete the Dahlia Playground at Althaus Park. Pending Hamilton County Commission approval, the funding will support the purchase of inclusive play equipment, creating a space where children of all abilities can play side by side.
 Inspired by Dahlia “Dollie” Harmon and her family’s vision for a more inclusive community, the project is another example of what strong public-private partnerships can accomplish for Hamilton County families.
 If approved on August 5, construction is expected to begin later this year. 🛝

items.title: RECALL ALERT: General Mills Issues Recall for Nearly 736,000 Pillsbury Rolls due to potential glass contamination. The i...
items.content_text:
RECALL ALERT: General Mills Issues Recall for Nearly 736,000 Pillsbury Rolls due to potential glass contamination.
 The issue was discovered internally. The cases are marketed to businesses rather than individual consumers.
 The recall includes 3,080 cases of Pillsbury Bread Rolls "Hard Roll Dough," each containing 180 rolls, for a total of 554,400 rolls, and 1,260 cases of Pillsbury Bread Rolls "Kaiser Roll Dough," each containing 144 rolls, for a total of 181,440 rolls.
 If you think you may have purchased either case of rolls, confirm by checking the UPC number on the package and the use-by date by visiting https://www.accessdata.fda.gov/scripts/ires/?Event=99293

items.title: Happy Friday, Chattanooga! ICYMI, It also happens to be Parks and Outdoors Day! We're incredibly lucky to live in a plac...
items.content_text:
Happy Friday, Chattanooga! ICYMI, It also happens to be Parks and Outdoors Day!
 We're incredibly lucky to live in a place where you can wrap up the workweek and immediately hit a hiking trail, launch a kayak, or just hang out under the trees at one of our many parks without leaving town. 
 Behind every beautiful green space, youth sports league, and outdoor adventure (just to name a few!)  is a massive team of people working their tails off. Our entire department is out there every single day doing the heavy lifting behind the scenes to keep this city green, safe, and active. We really can't thank our staff enough for the long hours and hard work they put in to make Chattanooga such an incredible place to live.
 But honestly, all that hard work doesn't mean much without you. A park is just empty grass until the community shows up. Thank you to everyone who fills up our neighborhood spaces, walks the greenways, plays on the courts, or just takes a quiet minute to sit by the river after a long week. Happy Friday, Chattanooga! ICYMI, It also happens to be Parks and Outdoors Day!
We're incredibly lucky to live in a place where you can wrap up the workweek and immediately hit a hiking trail, launch a kayak, or just hang out under the trees at one of our many parks without leaving town.
Behind every beautiful green space, youth sports league, and outdoor adventure (just to name a few!) is a massive team of people working their tails off. Our entire department is out there every single day doing the heavy lifting behind the scenes to keep this city green, safe, and active. We really can't thank our staff enough for the long hours and hard work they put in to make Chattanooga such an incredible place to live.

items.content_text:
What a week! The 10th cohort of the  Urban95 Academy has just concluded their residence week. 

In the past 5-years the programme has brought over 300 urban leaders representing 100 city teams from all over the world to London to study at the London School of Economics and Political Science (LSE).

29 urban officials from 10 cities arrived in London to take part in five days of intensive in-person teaching, workshops and site visits during the week-long residence in central London.

Read more about the week here : https://lnkd.in/eiaeVkr5

items.title: Nooga Has Heart: Youth Performance and Pathways is a talent competition for Chattanooga's next generation of performing...
items.content_text:
Nooga Has Heart: Youth Performance and Pathways is a talent competition for Chattanooga's next generation of performing artists!
 Produced by Fallon Clark, Executive Director of Noogavision Theatre and Performing Arts Co. and member of the 2026 cohort of the Emerging Producers Program, Nooga Has Heart aims to create access, visibility, and opportunity for emerging artists across many performing arts disciplines. 
 Young artists ages 13-21 are invited to audition! Selected participants will receive a spot to perform at Youth Performance & Pathways, participate in artist development workshops, have chances to connect with local arts professionals, and compete for the inaugural Performing Artist Pulse Awards. 
 Auditions close on June 30, 2026 at 11:59 PM. Learn more at www.noogavisiontheatre.org 
 Photo Credit: Fallon Clark, Noogavision Theatre and Performing Arts Co.

items.title: Still not over how unreal this project is 🔥🔥The Environmental and Economic Infrastructure Improvements project, or e2i...
items.content_text:
Still not over how unreal this project is 🔥🔥
The Environmental and Economic Infrastructure Improvements project, or e2i2, will capture and store excess wastewater from heavy rainfall in Chattanooga, Tennessee.
 We're bringing the City of Chattanooga’s infrastructure investment to life through a progressive design-build model. Proud to work alongside the City of Chattanooga, Hamilton County, Jacobs, Gresham Smith, Barge Design Solutions, Croy Engineering, and Terracon Consultants to help deliver cleaner water to the community.
 Get an inside look in this Local 3 News feature: https://www.local3news.com/…/article_c1e88e43-569e-4b4d-80e…

items.title: To: Mayor Lloyd and Collegedale City Commissioners From: Jon Holmes, City Manager Date: 07/17/2026 RE: Weekly Update 06/...
items.content_text:
To: Mayor Lloyd and Collegedale City Commissioners
 From: Jon Holmes, City Manager
 Date: 07/17/2026
 RE: Weekly Update 06/22 – 06/26
 Administration:
 This Weekly Update contains information from last week in addition to the current week since a weekly update was not distributed last Friday due to the City Manager being out of the office to attend a Conference
 • City Manager attended the TN Municipal League Conference along with two Commissioners, Commissioner Howse and Commissioner Sadler.
 • Public Works Staff, Police Staff, Parks and Rec Staff and Admin Staff After Event Meeting to discuss the Freedom Fest and talk through issues and concerns and how to best address those items next year.
 • Requested feedback from Commissioners on issues or concerns they saw during Freedom Fest, and any feedback they received from constituents.
 • CM also met with East Tennessee Symphony Orchestra to discuss their event and get feedback from them on concerns or issues and how to address them in preparation for discussions with the Commission to set the dates for next year’s event
 • Worked with Parks and Recreation Manager to assemble a survey for the community to gather input and opinions about the Freedom Fest event.
 • Met with two developers regarding possible proposed retail developments within Collegedale
 • Met with City Attorney regarding various items
 • Organized Joint Training for the City Commission, Planning Commission, and Economic(Industrial) Development Board for information about the Infrastructure Development Districts economic development program that is authorized by state statute
 • The new Communications and Engagement Manager for the City, McKenna Wallace, will begin her duties on August 3rd; we will be posting information about McKenna next week and we look forward to working with her.
 • Contacted the State Tourism Office to inquire about the Tourism Enhancement Grant – this grant has been used by municipalities around the state to assist with the installation of wayfinding and community signage in their respective communities. The next round of funding will be announced soon and we are planning on submitting an application to assist with our budgeted wayfinding signage project.
 Collegedale Airport:
 • We responded to audit requests from TDOT for our yearly grant compliance.
 • We had only a procedural finding for a Master Services Agreement with our previous on-call engineer consultant, that expired last year. The contract did not contain required language for subcontracting services. We are pleased to report that operationally, everything else was found to be in compliance. 
 • We have been following up on requests from TDOT regarding a previously issued NPDES permit that was given for the last obstruction removal project a few years back, to verify if it is still active. If it isn’t, we will need to apply for a re-issuance. There are some loose ends to get sorted out before the obstruction removal can begin that we are working through. We have been working with Michael Baker International and TDOT Aeronautics on a State-wide Obstruction Clearing initiative that was applied for a few weeks ago. An environmental assessment was submitted to TDEC and we’re currently waiting for a response to proceed.
 • The Airport responded to some reports of issues with the McDonald Terrace gate malfunctioning. The issue appears to be addressed.
 • We submitted the approved work authorization for the PAPI design project. We currently are waiting on the grant paperwork to come through so we can present it to the Commission for
 approval.
 • We have been working with our auditors to answer some contractual related questions.
 Building Department:
 Building Permits
 • (2) Electrical-new home
 • (2) Gas-new home
 • (2) Mechanical-new home
 • (2) Plumbing-new home
 • (3) ROW-new residence
 • (1) Building-accessory structure
 • (3) Building-new home
 • (1) Building-commercial warehouse
 • (1) Electrical-renovation/repair
 • (1) Electrical-new home
 • (1) Land Disturbance-warehouse
 • (2) Mechanical-new home
 • (3) Sewer-new home
 • (4) Sign-business
 Codes Enforcement:
 • Followed up with a homeowner regarding the contractor failing to secure the required construction permit.
 • Made contact with the new business owner of a vacant property and advised him of the existing violations on the property.
 • Notice of violation sent to the owner of property that has debris in the yard and several unresolved housing violations.
 • Provided information on the right of way boundaries for a business owner who was trying to determine approximately where his property begins in relation to the roadway.
 • Responded to several possible code violations reported by Public Works Department.
 • Spoke with a citizen who came to the office and had questions regarding permit requirements.
 • Followed up on accumulation of trash and debris violations. All violations were corrected and the locations are now in compliance.
 • Completed FOIA request for various code cases in Collegedale, TN for the month of June 2026.
 • Spoke with citizen and explained the reason she received a courtesy notice for accumulation of trash and debris.
 • Responded to possible code violations reported by Public Works Department.
 • Followed up on a citizen complaint of a prohibited parking violation.
 • Followed up with a several property owners regarding accumulation of trash and debris violations.
 • Meet with a citizen at the office who had questions regarding a recent courtesy notice she received.
 • Reviewed and updated AXON body camera footage information.
 • Returned a call to an out-of-town owner of Collegedale property who was requesting additional time to bring her property into compliance. 
 • Returned a phone call to a citizen complaining of illegal dumping.
 • Notice of violation sent to the owners of two short term vacation rental properties currently in violation.
 • Followed up on a citizen complaint of overgrown grass/weeds.
 • Followed up with a business owner regarding erecting a fence on his property.
 • Patrolled various streets within Collegedale
 • Reviewed and updated AXON body camera footage information.
 • Patrolled various subdivisions and streets within city limits.
 New/Opened Code Violation Cases:

 Katie Lamb Public Library:
 • This week we welcomed 1,720 visitors who checked out a total of 3,651 items.
 Upcoming Programs:
 • July 18 at 3:00 PM join us for Crime Reads Book Club to discuss Ruby Falls.
 • July 19 at 2:00 PM join us to hear from Local Author J. Kasper Kramer.
 Collegedale Parks and Recreation:
 Collegedale Parks & Recreation have the following upcoming programs:
 • 7/30 Creek Explorers
 • 7/30 Thursday Night Threads
 • 7/31 End of Summer Reading Party w/ Library
 • 8/5 Learn to Paddleboard (Ages 8+)
 • 8/6 Silent Disco
 • 8/6 Monthly Game Night
 • 8/21-8/23 Collegedale TimberFest
 •9/11 25th Anniversary of 9/11 "We Remember"
 • 9/20 Fairytale Fall Ball
 • 10/6 National Night Out
 • 10/11 Power Wheel Derby
 • 10/24 HES Vaccine Clinic
 • 10/27 Fall Carnival
 Planning Department & Community Development:
 • New City Planner Director, Garrett Hayes will begin his duties on July 27th; we welcome him and we are excited to work with him here in Collegedale.
 • Staff addressed routine day-to-day planning activities, responded to constituent inquiries, processed development-related items, and completed various departmental administrative and cleanup tasks.
 • CM worked with Planning Admin Asst. on a development application submittal.
 Collegedale Police Department:
 • Detective Watson is attending a sexual assault investigation class in Jacksonville, Florida this week.
 • Members of the Admin team worked on the new rifle range.
 • First round interviews were held for two days this week for selected candidates who had applied to be hired.
 Collegedale Public Works:
 • Prepared and set-up for, then cleaned up after Freedom Fest
 • Re-mulched Thatcher Switch and Imagination Station playgrounds
 • Collected illegally dumped items on Cemetery Road
 • Cleared and mowed sewer easements
 • Repaired water line break at Thatcher Switch bathrooms
 • Prepared and filed 2nd Quarter report with TDEC for transfer station
 • Reviewed applications for Public Works laborer positions
 • Pump Station progress
 • Start-up testing rescheduled due to valve leakage
 • Final building touch-up and site clean-up in progress
 • Weekly mowing of City Properties • Weekly Summary Thursday, July 9 – Wednesday, July 15
 • Dug up and repaired water leak at the Nature Nook
 • Performed repairs to undermined sidewalk on Ooltewah-Ringgold Road
 • Pump station progress
 • Final electrical connections/wiring in progress
 • Modifications between suction piping and new pumps completed
 • Weekly mowing of City Properties
 Upcoming Meetings and Events:
 July 20th – City Commission Regular Session Meeting
 July 27th – City Commission Workshop Session Meeting
 August 3rd – City Commission Regular Session Meeting
